videos.title: Best Things To Do in Panama City, Panama 4K
videos.description:
PANAMA CITY, PANAMA: In this list of the best things to do in Panama City, we show you around the top attractions in Panama. If you plan to visit Panama City and are looking for the best tourist places and destinations to explore while in Panama then this Panama City travel vlog is for you. We just created a list of 25 must do activities for you to do while visiting Panama City, Panama! In this Panama City travel guide we show you around downtown Panama City attractions, Casco Viejo, Avenida Balboa, Taboga Island, Panama Canal Miraflores, Bocas Del Toro, San Blas,  Monkey Island, Colon and much more! 

We also discuss Panama City safety, travel tips and the best place to stay in Panama City.

Top Things To Do in Panama City
00:00 Intro
00:36 Casco Viejo
01:25 San Blas
01:33 Bocas Del Toro
01:44 Indigenous Tribe
01:51 Isla Taboga
02:00 Visit Colon
02:08 Avenida Balboa
02:23 Amador Causeway
03:05 Bio Museum
03:47 Panama Canal
04:30 Food
04:56 Nightlife
05:19 Casino
05:33 Mercado Mariscos
06:25 Monkey Island
08:01 Metropolitan Park
09:02 Albrook Mall
09:59 Panama Vieja
12:03 City Bus Tour
12:28 Bridge Of The America's
13:07 Outro

Island Hopper TV takes you around the world to the premiere travel destinations. Our virtual travel guides from across America to Asia, Europe and Australia have assisted many world travelers in educating themselves on their next destination to travel or even vacation.

videos.title: The Don'ts of Visiting Panama City, Panama
videos.description:
Thinking about visiting Panama City, Panama? Before you head down to explore the Panama Canal, wander the colorful streets of Casco Viejo, or dig into incredible Panamanian food, make sure you know the DON’Ts of visiting Panama City so you can have a safer, smoother, and more enjoyable trip.

In this video, I break down the biggest mistakes tourists make in Panama City — from safety slip-ups in the old town and mishandling taxis to restaurant etiquette, food misunderstandings, and missing out on the best day trips like Monkey Island, Panama Canal locks, Biomuseo, Ancon Hill, and San Blas.

We talk about the neighborhoods to be careful in, the don’ts of walking around Casco Viejo, why you shouldn’t assume all seafood is fresh, which areas of the city are safe for tourists, and what NOT to do when visiting the Panama Canal or heading out to the islands.

If you want honest travel advice about Panama City from someone who’s been there, eaten there, and explored it top to bottom, this video will help you avoid the mistakes, scams, and frustrations that new visitors run into.

Whether you’re planning a long layover, a weekend getaway, or building Panama City into a longer Panama trip, these tips will help you travel smarter, save money, and stay safe.

👍 Don’t forget to LIKE, SUBSCRIBE, and drop a comment if you’ve been to Panama City or are planning your first trip!

Filmed in Panama City, Panama
#panama #visitpanama #panamacity

I'm just saying you need to be ready cuz if you're expecting those na prices, you're not going to get them. So, if you're going to be staying, especially here in Kasco Viejo, the old town, hotels are going to be pricier. Restaurants, I mean, oh, $25 for ceviche, it can happen. So be ready for a bit pricier stay when you're in Panama City. Now if you stay outside of Costco VJO, you're by the highrises, you get better prices and stuff, but just be aware of that because yeah, some tourists come to think that it's going to be, oh, super cheap Central America. Yeah, I it's not. Okay, be ready for that. And with those high prices, maybe you expect like really good service. Well, I got to tell you this. Don't expect stellar quick service when you do come to Panama City. you need to be a bit more proactive in your ordering and getting your bill than you do in other places. Um, so do be aware of that. So, if you're going out to eat, you know, do realize it'll take some time to [music] get your menu. It'll take some time for them to take your drink order, to bring you your drinks, to get your food. And I've seen a number of tourists get a little upset about that. Various restaurants around Panama City. Look, here things just take a little bit more time. Remember, it's Central America time. You just got to have a little more calmo kind of things. You'll be okay. But just know if you're expecting quick, fast, like, let's go, let's go, let's go. you know that that that happens in the Panama Canal. When you're around the city, it's more chillaxed and I've seen tourists get [music] a little upset. Also, I found that here in Panama City, you get less of the uh helpful locals than you do in other parts of Panama. So, do be aware of that, but it's, you know, the typical big city kind of thing. And even though I [music] said that the service isn't that good, you didn't know about tipping. Also, of note, when you're in Panama City, I mean, we're in Panama, you do tip. So, tip your guides, tip your drivers, you have that. But also, when you go to restaurants, you tip 10% minimum. That's like the thing. Now, in Pamel City, it's nice. They'll literally give you it 10, 15, or 20% so you know what to pay cuz it all gets put together. So, there's that. But, make sure you look cuz sometimes tipping isn't there or might already be included. So, just take a look and make sure you got your 10% tip there. All right? So, just know that, okay? Panama is a tipping country. Now, a nice thing when you're coming here, if you're coming from the US, is you don't have to exchange money. Look, here in Panama, they use the US dollar. Now, you might see, oh, wait, I thought they use the balboa. They do, but the only time you see balboas [music] are in coin form. So, and keep those for the metro. I'll talk about that later. But honestly, you can use US dollars. So, bring money when you're here. Don't forget to have small bills. So, if you're going to buy small little things for the change things, it makes life a little bit easier. But you don't have to exchange money, which is very nice. So, avoid those ATM fees. And there are plenty of ATMs here. Don't worry about that. Um, but make sure you don't bring your US dollars [music] with you. So, you already have it taken care of, which is cool. Now, moving to another topic. Let's talk about how you dress when you come here. I got to tell you, uh, don't think you're going to get away from the heat, humidity, and bugs when you're here in Panama City because look, Panama is famous for the heat and humidity. It's famous for the mosquitoes and the little noiums kind of things. You're not going to get away from it. So, don't forget to bring good sunblock, have the bug spray, have the bug wipes, and if you have them, make sure you have the [music] afterbite cuz they'll still get you whether you're going to the canal, whether you're in the city. It's going to be one of those things. So, don't think you're going to get away from that. Prepare for that. Have your hat. I have mine in my bag. But another thing I think it's important to talk about when we're talking about clothing here is uh don't forget you don't wear your beach clothes into town. But remember this isn't a beach city [music] so it's not a big deal. But I would say this, don't dress like a slob in Panama City. When you go around, you know, people are dressed a little bit nicer. I would say like 20% more collar than your usual Latin America city. That would be the [music] best way I kind of describe it. So do make sure you bring some collared shirts when you come cuz you know it is one of those things you'll notice when you're here. So when you're here in Panama City, don't forget you have to recharge Real Your Pass here. So you go buy your pass and they have these machines here. [music] You click Real and then you choose you want you want to pay with coins or betes which [music] is bills. So you do one of those two. So you have that very simple, very cheap, takes you like a lot of place you might want to go when you're here if you're going to Costco. So CO de Mayo is the closest metro stop. It's about maybe a mile kilometer and a bit maybe from uh from the like Blaza Benencia, but just know very easy to use. Works all over. Easy recharge. Don't forget them. Also, don't think you have to get cards for everybody. You can have one card which will charge each time you want to use it for each person. So, I have one card so it works for both me and Liam. So, don't worry about getting too many. Just one. Recharge it and go. So, another I have for you. If you notice Liam over there, he's on a very high curb. Don't think all the curbs are the same height. I mean, it can be a big drop off and when you walk around, it is not, you know, handicap [music] accessible. So, do be aware of that because there's a lot of uneven pavement here. And I've seen a few tourists bite it while we've been here. So, be careful with that. Now, another I want to talk about is one that doesn't happen often, but when it happens, you'll know about it. And that's the national strikes. And I want to tell you, don't worry too much about getting to the airport when there's one of those national strikes. Because usually what happens with the national strikes, they actually close down the Pan-Amean Highway and that is not going [music] from here in Kasco VJO or the highrises out to the airport. Okay? So, you'll be okay. But I know some people get really scared because sometimes they close down those the Pan-American for a long long time. So, you might be worried at the airport, but don't worry about that. You'll be okay. And I think what'll make you feel a little bit better about that is uh don't forget to brush up on your Spanish before you go to Panama City. Yes, lots of tourists come here. [music] This is one of the more Englishspoken places in Central America. But it never hurts to know some of the Spanish when you come here cuz a buenos das like good day when you go into a store. [music] Gracias, which is thank you goes a long way when you're here cuz the people appreciate it. They don't want rude tours being like straight in English. But um don't worry if you don't speak Spanish you do come here cuz the menus, look, wherever you're going to go, most the menus will have English versions on it as well and not a separate menu. I just a menu in Spanish and has English underneath. So check that out so you can have some of the local food when you're here. The thing I will tell you though, um, don't expect to have a lot of local Panameanian restaurants. You'll notice when you're over in the highrises, you'll see a lot of international food, [music] Chinese food, Indian food, Italian food. You got to look for some of the Panameanian restaurants. So do be aware of that. But don't miss out on some of the treats when you are here. The Panameanian treats you want to have. Lots of little things in panadas, little fried dough stuff you can have, which is really good. But also paragraph which is the uh fried fried red snapper is really good. Rob viea which is basically shredded beef is really good. Uh chicha which [music] is basically like think about just juices made from like local fruits and stuff or corn is really good. So you have a lot of options if you're here. But if you do get a little tired of panimmanian food, if you've been here for a couple weeks, you probably will get tired of paninian food. You have a lot of other options as well. >> [music] [music] >> One other note I have for you when you are here in Panama City, don't expect people to stop for you when you cross the street. So don't be afraid when the cops are helping you cross the street. They're there to help you. So do be aware of that because pedestrians do not have the rideway or at least you will notice they don't have the rideaway regard. This is what the law is. So, heads up on that one. I will say, don't pass up coming to the Marcato, the Morisos, the seafood market. It's so cool to see all the fresh fish that's here. There's also restaurants you can eat at right by to have really super fresh ceviche and everything. So, if you are here, just come check it out. It's fantastic. And if you're cooking here by yourself, you can pick up dinner here as well and cook it at home. But yeah, the vero, the maris is a must when you are here. Now, when you come to Panama, rain is going to come. So, I want to tell you this. Don't think it doesn't rain in the dry season because it does rain here. But I will say it is important to take care of when you are going to come because the highest point of the rainy season like in October, I don't really recommend coming [music] because you're going to get rain a lot of the times, most times during the day. But honestly, when you do come, even if you're here like I'm here in the dry dry season and you have a little downpour that'll pop up, have a little poncho with you. It's usually pretty quick and goes away. But yeah, don't think dry season means dry, no rain season, because it'll get you. If you're in Panama, don't ever doubt Panamanian pride because you'll see those flags flying everywhere. And Liam's right. They are probably one of the coolest flags you can see in the Western Hemisphere.

videos.title: A Beginner’s Guide To Panama City (What To Know Before You Go)
videos.description:
Imagine sipping rooftop cocktails above a skyline that feels like Miami met Dubai, and then wandering 10 minutes into cobblestone plazas where colonial ruins meet salsa bands. Oh, and yes, you might see a sloth hanging in someone’s backyard.

This is Panama City, Central America’s most surprising capital. A mashup of finance towers, iguana walkers, rooftop bars, and the canal that literally changed the world.

Here’s what’s inside this guide:
🏙️ Casco Viejo – cobblestone chaos, colonial churches & rooftop cocktails
🚢 The Panama Canal – ships, locks & capitalism in motion
🌳 Nature in the city – monkeys, sloths & skyline views from Ancon Hill
🍤 Food you can’t skip – ceviche, hojaldras & Michelin-level fondas
🏨 Where to stay – from boutique Casco hotels to crypto-millionaire marble
🌅 The vibe – why Panama City is equal parts glamorous & gritty
▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬
🔥PREMIUM BEACH TOWELS🔥
Shop our premium 100% cotton Turkish beach towels now: https://gobopp.com
▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬
👨 ABOUT 👨
On the Easy Travel Dude channel we give you island and coastal travel content. We hope to inspire you to travel more. Alone, with friends or your family.
▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬
🔔SUBSCRIBE! 🔔
Subscribe for more travel content.
Subscribe ► https://www.youtube.com/channel/UCtkP7_BBrUkaC7xRrxFLzGA 
▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬

The information on this YouTube channel and the resources available are for entertainment, educational and informational purposes only.​
▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬

videos.transcript: Imagine [Music] this. You're sipping rooftop cocktails in a glittering skyline that looks like Miami had a love child with Dubai. And 10 minutes later, you're in a crumbling colonial plaza watching a man walk an iguana on a leash. Welcome to Panama City, a place where billionaires bank in skyscrapers, sloths hang out in the suburbs, and the canal that changed the world. charges ships six figures just to cut in line. Stick around because we're diving deep into the city that might just blow your mind and then politely charge you a toll to put it back together. [Music] Let's get one thing straight. Panama City is not what you think it is. Yes, it's in Central America. No, it's not a sleepy little village with hammocks and coconuts. This city slaps hard. You've got shiny highrises built by international banking giants. You've got Casco Viejo, the old quarter with Spanish ruins, rooftop bars, and murals that practically flirt with you. You've got the Cinta Costa, a seaside prominade where joggers, street food vendors, and pelicans coexist in cardio harmony. And if you think that's a lot, we haven't even gotten to the monkeys. Casco Viejo is where you come for cobblestone chaos, colonial churches, rooftop happy hours, and possibly a salsa band that appears out of nowhere. This is the soul of Panama City. Pastel buildings with peeling paint. Local kids playing soccer at sunset. Craft cocktail bars with menus longer than a dsttoyki novel. Pro tip, watch the sunset from Tantelo rooftop bar while locals debate politics and tourists pretend to know what ceviche actually is. You can't say you've done Panama City without a date with the Panama Canal. Go to the Mirror Floors locks and watch multi-million dollar cargo ships pass through a system that's been operating since 1914 and still runs like clockwork. It's nerdy. It's jaw-dropping. It's capitalism at its most aquatic. And yes, you will leave googling how to buy a container ship. And hey, if you're craving beaches where skyscrapers meet the sea, check out our guide to Panama's beaches next. From golden sands just outside Panama City to hidden Pacific and Caribbean gems, it's the perfect escape when the city buzz gets too loud. What other capital city lets you go hiking in a national park 10 minutes from downtown? Park Natural Metropolitano is your rainforest fix. Sloths, toucans, howler monkeys, and skyline views that make it feel like the jungle's about to merge with the boardroom. Want something wilder? Head to Anconill for the best panoramic view of the city, plus a bonus flag the size of a studio apartment. Panameanian food is criminally underrated. You've got ropa vieja, shredded beef stewed in magic. Hoaldras, fried dough that will ruin all other breakfast carbs. And fresh seafood that could make Poseidon wheat. Try Marcato de Marisos for ceviche straight out of the sea. Or head to Fondali for nextgen local cuisine that feels like your abuela got a Michelin star. [Music] Want the view without the financial trauma? Check out Hotel LaMa in Casco. Historic, chic, and walkable. Ball in on a budget? Try Selena Casco Vo. Part hostel, part co-working, part how do these people have abs and still drink so much? And if you want to pretend you're a cryptoillionaire for a night, Waldorf Histori has your name written in marble. Panama City doesn't try to win you over. It challenges you. It's rich and raw, glamorous and gritty, a mashup of global finance and local street food. A city that makes you ask, "What even is the vibe here?" And then answers, "All of them." You don't come here to relax. You come to feel alive, slightly confused, and maybe a little sunburnt. So, ready to fall in love with the city that built a canal and a personality to match? Hit subscribe for more wild, weird, wonderful guides. And if Panama just upgraded your travel list, let us know below. See you in Casco. I'll be the one trying to salsa with a churro in hand. [Music]

videos.title: 7 Things You Should Know Before Visiting Panama 🇵🇦
videos.description:
Planning a trip to Panama City, Panama? ✈️ Before you pack your bags, here are 7 important things you should know that will make your visit smoother, cheaper, and way more enjoyable.

In this video, I’ll cover:
1️⃣ The weather (why the dry season is the best time to visit) ☀️
2️⃣ Rental car insurance & what to watch out for 🚗
3️⃣ Setting up your phone service 📱
4️⃣ Why learning basic Spanish phrases is a must 🗣️
5️⃣ What to expect when budgeting for food, activities, and housing 💵
6️⃣ The quality of internet services in Panama 🌐
7️⃣ Entry requirements (yes—you’ll need a return or onward ticket) 🛂

Panama City is a vibrant mix of modern life, history, and natural beauty. Knowing these tips ahead of time will help you avoid surprises and enjoy every moment of your trip.

👉 Don’t forget to like 👍, comment 💬, and subscribe 🔔 for more Panama travel tips, expat insights, and lifestyle content!

#PanamaCity #TravelTips #PanamaTravel #VisitPanama #ExpatsInPanama #CentralAmericaTravel #TravelGuide #food #realestate
videos.transcript: [Music] Hello. Hello everyone. and thank you for joining me today. Today we are going to talk about seven things that you need to know before moving to Panama. If you are new here, my name is Latria Hill. I moved to Panama a year and a half ago and I still absolutely love it here. So, let's get into the seven things that you must know before visiting Panama. So, number one would be when is the best time to visit Panama? Panama has two seasons. It has a rainy season and a dry season. In my opinion, I love the wet season, but this season it has been raining almost every day, guys. So, if you are an outdoors person, you want to do hiking, biking, taking a couple outdoor tours on the ocean, you might want to come during the dry season. So, in my opinion, the best time to come to Panama would be December through April. That is the dry season here in Panama. Number two would be transportation. When I booked my ticket to come to Panama, I had no idea how much uh car rentals would be. After looking for it online, I was so excited. Like, oh my god, this is so cheap. I can have a whole car for a month for $600. I was excited. But once I got there to get my car, the insurance that you have to have here in Panama is through the roof when it comes to rental cars. So for my $600, my insurance was about $1,200 that I had to pay on my card to get my car. They will not let you um get the car without the insurance. Whenever you see someone new coming in town at that rental car place, they're so upset because nobody tells you this. So, number two is just know that the insurance on a rental car is going to be through the roof. Nine times out of 10, it's going to be probably uh twice as much as the rental car fee. But secondly, if you didn't want to uh drive, all you needed to do was just Uber. Uber here is extremely cheap. From the airport, you can probably catch an Uber into the city for about $20. Anywhere in the city, you can probably get an Uber for about two, two, three, four dollars. So Uber here is pretty much what we use to get around. I do have a car, so I just drive everywhere. But when I'm in like Costco, VJO, or certain parts of the city, I don't drive in. I'll just Uber on in because it just makes things easier. But that's number two, guys. Just knowing that when you rent that car, the insurance is going to be twice as much as your car rental. Number three would be your phone plan. Have a plan for your phone before you get here. Um because a lot of times people get off that plane and have zero service. So we do use WhatsApp here. There is a um Wi-Fi connection that you can connect to to the airport once you get here, but once you're outside of that airport, your phone probably will not work. So, I would say um I wouldn't get a Sims card there in the airport. I would wait until I'm in the city. Um probably like the first gas station or mini mart or something like that. I would get the SIM card there. But you definitely want to have a plan for your cell phone because it will not work if you don't have that international plan or if you do if you don't have an international plan and are able to uh dial out you you'll be charged. I mean my first month here I was charged like $10 a day. So be sure that you have a plan for your cell phone. So that's number three guys. Number four would be the language. The language that they speak here in Panama is Spanish. So, if you do not speak Spanish, you will have um a difficult time communicating with some of the locals and um on some of the excursions that you might decide to go on. I don't speak Spanish Spanish, but I'm learning. And I could see where if I would have learned a little bit of etiquette and some restaurant Spanish before I got here, I would be able to order the dishes that I love. I would be able to ask the questions that I need if I'm lost, um, or if I'm hurt. So, some of the key things like that, you might want to learn how to say some key phrases in Spanish before you get here to make your transition a little bit easier because a lot of the Panamaeanians, they don't speak English or they speak broken English. So, it'll be very hard to communicate, but you can. But if you have some time, I would just say go ahead and learn some key phrases that will help you along once you get here. Number five would be the cost. How much should I bring when I come to Panama? I think that just depends on what you're planning on doing. The excursions here will run you from $40 to $200 for pretty much all of the nicer ones that you might want to do. If you do want to travel the country to see different cities and things and you might want to hire a driver, that can cost you about $60. But I would come with cash. You can bring up until $10,000 cash in with you into Panama. Even if you don't want to bring that much, I mean, not for a vacation or a scouting trip, but I came with a bunch of cash. I came with it of the things that I wanted to do. When I first came here, I just traveled as a tourist. I wanted to see Panama. I wanted to see all the bells and whistles of Panama. And I think that first month cost me about I would say about $2,000 in excursions for the entire month. And um I overspent for my Airbnb because I just felt that if I spend more money, it was going to be safer. So do not make that mistake. Panama is pretty safe overall. Um all of the different areas, I haven't had a problem. I haven't seen a problem here. So, I wouldn't get too hung up on um if this costs this amount, maybe it's better because I kind of did that. I wanted to get into the highrises to have the security, but the majority of the places do have security already. You don't have to worry about it. And obviously, if you're going into a hotel, you will not have that issue. But I wouldn't get so caught up in spending more when you can pretty much do it for less as far as your living is concerned here. So, the cost of living, the food, um, you can go cheap, you can go to the fondas, you can go $5, three, $4 up into $15 a plate some places. But if you're eating out, breakfast, lunch, and dinner, you might want to have a budget for that so that you can map out just about just about how much money you would need to bring here. I would say that only I would only do one excursion a day as well. Um, I tried to cram as much as I could in and I was exhausted. The weather here is hot. It's humid. So, I probably would just do one nice thing a day, let that soak in and then probably just walk around the city for the rest of the day. But, um, yeah, the cost of living here, the cost of the excursions are going to cost between 50 and $200 and the food between5 and $15 a plate. So depending on what you want to eat, how you want to eat would be the internet. A lot of people ask about the internet connections there in the city and outside of the city. You will not have a problem with the internet connection. The internet connection here are I mean it's pretty strong. I don't have a problem here and I'm out here in the country. The only time I do have a problem is with um if electricity goes out then obviously I'm going to have a problem. But outside of that, no problem. No problem in the city with um the internet. As long as your building has a backup generator, you won't have any problems because if the power goes off, it's just going to shut right back on within two seconds. So, it will not have any interference. So, coming to Panama, you do not have to worry about the internet connections. A lot of people do come here. They work. They uh the internet connection is not an issue here. Number seven, guys, coming into Panama, you will need a return flight. So, you would need your return flight going back home or you would need a forwarding flight taking you to your next destination. But here in Panama, once you reach Panama, they will ask you, they will look for it and want to know your return flight. So, I hope this helps you not make the same mistakes that I made when I first got here. I completely overspent my first month in Panama. I think overall between the $2,000 in excursions, my Airbnb ended up being about $3,000. And so total about $5,000 for the month of April, my first trip to Panama. So you do you guys do not have to spend that much. Like I said, the safety, it's safe anywhere you stay here in Panama for the most part. I I would not be concerned about that. I would not even overpay for food. There's some nice restaurants that you can stay in that range between$9 and $10 a day. But if you do want to go a little bit more fancy, you can get the ones that are 15 to $20 a um I think I said a day a meal. So enjoy Panama, guys. I hope that you love it as much as I do. And if you have any questions, please leave them in the comments and I will respond to every one of them. Thank you and see you on the next video. Bye.

videos.title: Before You Visit Panama City! 🇵🇦 SAFETY Tips | #TravelVlog @getfitwithchrys
videos.description:
👉 Curious about visiting Panama City, Panamá, but wondering if it's safe?

Discover everything you need to know before visiting Panama City in this insightful video! We’ll explore safety tips, transportation options like Ubers and taxis, and share our personal experiences as travelers.

As a black female American solo traveler, we discuss how blending in with the local culture can enhance your experience. From modest dress codes to navigating the city’s vibrant streets, we cover essential insights that will help you feel comfortable during your visit. 🧳

Join us on this journey through Panama City! 🇵🇦 

#PanamaCity #TravelTips
#PanamaCitySafety #PanamaTravel #travelcentralamerica #panama #blackgirlmagic #travel #latinamerica #getfitwithchrys #panamacanal

[Music] God so let me tell you the coolest thing happened in the coffee shop so while I'm there um a young lady approached me and she was like hey I saw your video and I showed it to my mom who's retiring and I just thought that was so dope that someone in a whole different country saw me from YouTube and was like thank you for posting that video so shout out to Octavia who I met in the coffee shop and your mom safe travels to you both that just goes to show you never know who's watching so that was pretty cool to me especially as we grow this channel so I know I talked a lot about dressing and how you present yourself but honestly that's probably one of the main things that can keep you safe especially if if you are a tourist like you don't want to walk around with a bunch of flashy jewelry on you know if you're a woman make sure that you aren't wearing something that might cause you to get some attention that you might not want it's a popular saying in Latin America no daughter papaya which is like basically Don't Give A Reason don't make yourself a Target and be aware of your surroundings another thing in Panama City that I really love are the police engaging with the police here is completely different than engaging with them in the states when I first got here I was a little caught off guard because I see them heavily armed they got on all this like gear and it's like oh like are we good here but when you approach them they're very nice they're very friendly they may have you know weapons on them but they're really not trying to cause no issues and they could probably care less about what you got going on long as you ain't you know in no Shenanigans they're scattered out through the city so even if you're walking in an area that might not be so safe there might not speak the language or you're taking taxis or other corporate rid share forms of transportation like I said earlier in the video I've been taking Ubers so I feel safe taking Ubers because we get in you give them a pen obviously you want to check the license plate the same way that I would in the states so you can take a taxi I just can't speak on that and I do know there are transportation services but I honestly don't feel like you need it here like the Ubers are like $2 $3 it does add up but it's not 20 it's not 30 and you can get pretty far for just a little bit of money um taking Ubers also another form of transportation is the Metro the subway their subway system is so clean I didn't feel unsafe um the the directions are easy to follow I'm not that great with directions but being on their Metro system is just it's very easy to get around and it takes you throughout the entirety of Panama City so that's another way that you can get around you do have to get like a little Metro Car so if you feel uncomfortable not knowing what to do that's something you may want to like look into but it's really just going in um paying for the card and then using the card to get onto the train um once you get there next up racism I did have some people ask about racism in Panama Panama City again I'm only been here for a couple months and so I can't really speak too much to that but I have spoken to people who are from here who live here um and I can speak from some of my own experiences there are some little things that you may recognize if you're a tourist or if you present to be a darker skinned Panamanian in some areas you can definitely tell that colorism is real if I go into an area that might be filled with lighter skinned panamanians I might get a little bit different treatment just depends on where you are and I've asked to confirm if this was true or if I was tripping and people who are from here say yes there are some microaggressions depending on the communities that you're in and although unfortunate um it is a reality in a lot of places you know it's not something I would think that you would be in danger for but you may recognize it I've recognized it just a little bit it hasn't really altered my experience here but it is something that you might pick up on depending on where you're from how you look and how you carry yourself in certain communities but overall like honestly I think being black is an advantage here it's easy to blend in I don't feel ostracized at all I actually feel like I belong here if I've had to put racism on a scale in terms of my experience here it would probably be like a 2 out of 10 everyone has a different experience so yeah that's that on that oh here's the thing that might be a disadvantage if you are a tourist because they might sense that you're a tourist and you have a certain amount of money or whatever it may be you may get uh jipped on the price I went to one of the local markets and they tried to charge me like $2 for two apples and if I didn't know that that was not a regular price I would have got got now $2 might not sound like a lot to you but if I know that those apples are only 25 and then you're purposefully trying to like upcharge me that's not fair right that's not right so make sure you like look around and understand the pricing of things so that you don't get got on anything really because they can make up their own prices and just charge you based on the fact that you don't look like you're from here um that goes with a lot of things like food um places to stay sometimes airbnbs like just look around do your research know the pricing of things before you buy stuff so that you can know that you're actually getting the real price and value of what you're buying other than that that's really it overall I believe Panama is a very safe City I felt so safe here I mean I've walked around by myself at night in the daytime it doesn't matter what time I have felt very very comfortable as a female Solo Traveler so if you're having any hesitancy about traveling um to Panama City I'm just going to say don't like just just come it's a great beautiful city with beautiful people and a lot to explore if you're from Panama City feel free to educate me in the comments I would love to hear your feedback and if you've been to Panama City
